did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and trained a custom convolutional neural network (CNN) from scratch for automated skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The study utilized deep learning techniques including preprocessing pipelines, data augmentation, and Grad-CAM for explainability to achieve a 94% accuracy in detecting skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where a custom convolutional neural network (CNN) was developed and trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. It reports specific quantitative results (94% accuracy, 95% CI: 93.0–95.0%) and compares them against baseline models (ResNet50, VGG16). Although published in a conference proceedings (IC2SDT), it contains original algorithmic development, training methodology, and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning-Based Skin Cancer Detection: A Comprehensive Approach for Automated Dermatological Diagnosis
journal: 2025 Second International Conference on Pioneering Developments in Computer Science & Digital Technologies (IC2SDT)
brief_summary: This study developed a custom convolutional neural network (CNN) trained from scratch to automate skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model incorporated a comprehensive preprocessing pipeline for artifact removal and contrast enhancement, alongside two-phase data augmentation to mitigate class imbalance across seven diagnostic categories. Evaluation on the test set demonstrated that the proposed architecture achieved an overall accuracy of 94%, outperforming baseline models such as ResNet50 and VGG16. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was utilized to provide explainability by highlighting diagnostically significant regions within the lesion images. The findings indicate that this lightweight, end-to-end deep learning approach offers a computationally efficient and accurate solution for computer-aided dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class in HAM10000 dataset with 6,705 images; majority class.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 1,113 images; malignant class.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 1,099 images.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 514 images; malignant class.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 327 images.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 142 images.
Class in HAM10000 dataset with 115 images; minority class.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a custom CNN for automated skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs class probabilities via a Softmax layer with 7 units corresponding to the seven lesion types. The task is explicitly described as 'multi-class evaluation' and 'classification of dermoscopic skin lesions'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A widely used benchmark dataset consisting of 10,015 dermatoscopic images divided into seven diagnostic classes. It was used as the primary source for training and evaluating the custom CNN model.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN from scratch with a 20% validation split from the training set.
use.validation: 20% of the training set was used for validation during training.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC-ROC).
use.other: Used to benchmark baseline models (VGG16, ResNet50) for comparison.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the statistical uncertainty around the overall accuracy metric (94.0%) for the proposed model.
To address class imbalance during model training by assigning different weights to different lesion classes.
To prevent overfitting by halting training when validation loss does not decrease for a specified number of epochs (patience=3).
To adjust the learning rate during training to aid convergence, applied when validation loss plateaus.
To stabilize training by limiting the magnitude of gradients (constraint ||∇θ L||2 ≤ 1.0).
To reduce overfitting by randomly setting 20% of neuron outputs to zero during training.
To stabilize and accelerate training by normalizing layer inputs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics with 95% confidence intervals for the proposed model and compared absolute performance values against baseline models (ResNet50, VGG16) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model outperformed baseline models (ResNet50 and VGG16) which were trained on the same dataset using identical preprocessing, augmentation, and weighted loss configurations. The paper did not report per-class metrics or standard deviations for the results.

architecture_details.backbone: Custom hierarchical CNN with four convolutional blocks
architecture_details.initialization: He Normal for Conv2D and Dense layers; Glorot Uniform for output layer

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduce-on-Plateau with decay factor 0.5 and patience 3 epochs
regularization: Batch Normalization after each convolutional and dense block

data_augmentation.offline: Geometric and photometric transformations for minority classes to reach 1000 samples each
data_augmentation.online: Random cropping/resizing, color jittering, random affine transformations, elastic deformations

preprocessing_techniques: Standardization using ImageNet mean (µ) and standard deviation (σ)
Hair removal using morphological black-hat filtering and inpainting
Contrast enhancement using CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority classes (those with fewer than 1000 samples) to synthetically augment them to at least 1000 images each. Specific transformations included: Random rotation (angles θ ∈ [0°, 360°]), Horizontal and vertical flips (probability p = 0.5), Random zooming (scaling factors s ∈ [0.8, 1.2]), Shearing (affine shear angle α ∈ [-15°, 15°]), and Brightness/contrast adjustments (modifications within ±10% of original values). This reduced the class imbalance ratio from 58:1 to approximately 6.7:1.

Applied during training on batches to improve robustness. Techniques included: Random cropping and resizing (crops of varying size and aspect ratio resized to 224x224), Color jittering (random perturbations in hue, saturation, and brightness modeled as additive noise in HSV color space), Random affine transformations (rotation, translation, scaling applied randomly per sample), and Elastic deformations (non-linear pixel shifts modeled as displacement fields to simulate lesion shape variations).

conclusions: The proposed custom convolutional neural network (CNN), trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset, achieved an overall accuracy of 94.0% (95% CI: 93.0–95.0%), sensitivity of 90.0%, specificity of 96.0%, and an AUC-ROC of 0.97.

The custom CNN outperformed baseline models ResNet50 (90.5% accuracy) and VGG16 (89.0% accuracy) when all models were trained with identical preprocessing, augmentation, and weighted loss configurations.

The model architecture is lightweight, containing approximately 1.28 million trainable parameters, offering a trade-off between expressiveness and computational efficiency suitable for potential real-time or mobile deployment.

Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was successfully utilized to provide explainability by highlighting diagnostically significant regions within lesion images, enhancing clinical interpretability.
A two-phase data augmentation strategy (offline for minority classes and online real-time) effectively mitigated class imbalance, reducing the imbalance ratio from 58:1 to approximately 6.7:1.

The study demonstrates that a carefully optimized, lightweight deep learning model trained from scratch can achieve strong performance in automated dermatological diagnosis without relying on transfer learning or ensemble methods.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (NV:DF ratio of 58:1) and demographic biases; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.

The custom CNN was trained from scratch without transfer learning, which is a strength for computational efficiency but may limit feature representation compared to models pretrained on large-scale datasets like ImageNet.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) or standard deviations, only overall accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC-ROC, limiting the ability to assess performance on minority classes like Dermatofibroma (115 samples).
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was conducted to compare the proposed model against baselines (ResNet50, VGG16); comparisons are based on absolute metric values.
The model was evaluated on a single test set split from HAM10000; results may not generalize to other populations or dermoscopic image acquisition devices due to lack of multi-center validation.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by using identical preprocessing and augmentation for all models, but the offline augmentation strategy (synthetically balancing classes to 1000 samples) may artificially inflate performance metrics compared to real-world imbalanced conditions.
The paper is published in a conference proceedings (IC2SDT), which may have less rigorous peer review standards compared to top-tier journals, warranting caution in clinical adoption.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (also referred to as ISIC 2018 in some contexts within the provided metadata, but explicitly named HAM10000 in the text) for all phases of the experiment. The dataset consists of 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven diagnostic classes. The custom CNN was trained from scratch on the training portion of this dataset, with 20% of the training set reserved for validation. The remaining portion served as the internal test set. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper explicitly states that the study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset. No external validation on independent datasets was performed. The results are based on a single test set split from HAM10000.

training.counts: Approximately 8,012 images (80% of 10,015 total, assuming standard split logic with 20% validation from training)
training.notes: Used for training the custom CNN from scratch. The dataset had a severe class imbalance (NV:DF ratio of 58:1) which was addressed via two-phase data augmentation.

validation.counts: Approximately 2,003 images (20% of the training set)
validation.notes: Used for model selection, early stopping (patience=3), and learning rate scheduling. It was a random split from the training data, not a separate held-out cohort.

testing.counts: Approximately 2,003 images (remaining portion of the dataset not used in training/validation)
testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. The paper reports overall metrics on this test set. Note: The exact test set size is not explicitly stated as a count, but derived from the total minus training/validation split. The paper states 'Test Set' in Table II.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used only for standardization (mean and standard deviation) of input pixels. The custom CNN was trained from scratch without using ImageNet weights for transfer learning. Baseline models (ResNet50, VGG16) likely used ImageNet pretraining, but the primary proposed model did not.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Offline augmentation was applied to minority classes (those with <1000 samples) to synthetically increase their count to at least 1000 images each. Online real-time augmentation was applied during training batches.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The paper states 'Validation split: 20% of the training set'. It does not explicitly state the training/test split ratio, but implies a standard hold-out test set from the remaining data after the validation split. Total images: 10,015.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set is a hold-out portion of the same HAM10000 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are overall metrics. Per-class metrics were not reported. The model outperformed baselines trained on the same data.
Baseline model trained with identical preprocessing and augmentation.
Baseline model trained with identical preprocessing and augmentation.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: 20% validation split from training set; remaining data used for testing.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and demographic biases.
No external validation on independent datasets was performed, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or dermoscopic image acquisition devices.
The offline augmentation strategy (synthetically balancing classes to 1000 samples) may artificially inflate performance metrics compared to real-world imbalanced conditions.
The paper is published in a conference proceedings (IC2SDT), which may have less rigorous peer review standards.

Per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) for the test set.
Standard deviations for the reported metrics.
